name: Pontianak Ghost
description: The Pontianak is a vampiric ghost in Malay folklore, believed to be the spirit of a woman who died during childbirth. She is often depicted as a beautiful woman with long black hair, pale skin, and sharp fingernails, but this beauty is a facade. A telltale sign of a Pontianak is a strong floral scent, often of frangipani or jasmine, which precedes her appearance. Her presence is also often accompanied by the sound of a baby crying. She seeks revenge for her untimely death and preys on men, especially those who are travelling alone or are perceived as vulnerable. The Pontianak is said to have a hole in the back of her neck, which is sometimes hidden by her long hair. If this hole is plugged with a nail, she can be tamed and become a good wife, but if the nail is removed, she will revert to her vengeful nature. She is a terrifying figure, embodying the pain and anger of a woman wronged.
subtitle: Vengeful Spirit of Childbirth
appearance: A beautiful woman with long, flowing black hair that conceals a hole in the back of her neck. She has pale skin, sharp fingernails, and piercing red eyes. She is often dressed in a white gown, stained with blood. Her beauty is an illusion, masking her true monstrous form.
habitat: She lurks in banana trees, abandoned buildings, and dark forests, particularly near cemeteries or places where women have died during childbirth.
behavior: The Pontianak lures victims with her beauty and sweet floral scent, often appearing as a damsel in distress. She preys on men, especially those who are alone or perceived as weak. She kills by tearing at their flesh with her sharp fingernails or by sucking their blood. She is driven by a desire for revenge and a deep-seated anger over her own death.
weaknesses: Nails (especially driven into the nape of the neck)
Sharp objects (e.g., scissors, knives) placed near newborns

keyMyths.title: The Origin of the Pontianak
keyMyths.description: The Pontianak is believed to originate from women who died during childbirth, becoming vengeful spirits seeking retribution for their untimely demise.  Their pain and anger transform them into monstrous beings preying on the living, especially men.

sources.author: Sir Richard Winstedt
sources.description: A classic study of Malay folklore and magic, including accounts of the Pontianak.
sources.text: A classic study of Malay folklore and magic, including accounts of the Pontianak.
